title: Mindfulness of Current Thoughts

From DBT Skills Training Handouts and Worksheets, Second Edition by Marsha M. Linehan. Copyright 2015 by Marsha M. Linehan. Permis-

Distress Tolerance Worksheet 12 (Distress Tolerance Handouts 15, 15a)
Mindfulness of Current Thoughts
Due Date: Name: Week Starting:
Describe your efforts to observe your thoughts in the past week. Practice observing thoughts each
day at least once. Don’t focus just on thoughts that are painful, anxiety-provoking, or full of anger;
also observe and be mindful of pleasant or neutral thoughts. For each thought, first practice saying,
“The thought [describe thought] went through my mind.” Then practice one or more strategies to
observe and let go of thoughts.
Check off any of the following exercises that you did.

 1. Used words and voice tone to say a thought over and over; as fast as I could; very, very
slowly; in a voice different from mine; as a dialogue on a TV comedy show; or as singing.

 2.	Relaxed my face and body imagining accepting my thoughts as sensations of my brain.

 3.	Imagined what I would do if I stopped believing everything I think.

 4.	Rehearsed in my mind what I would do if I did not view my thoughts as facts.

 5.	Practiced loving my thoughts as they went through my mind.

 6.	Refocused my mind on sensations I was avoiding by worrying or catastrophizing.

 7. Allowed my thoughts to come and go as I focused on observing my breath coming in and
out.

 8.	Labeled the thought as a thought, saying, “The thought [describe thought] arose in my
mind.”

 9.	Asked, “Where did the thought come from?” and watched my mind to find out.

 10. Stepped back from my mind, as if I was on top of a mountain.

 11. Shuttled back and forth between scanning for physical sensations and scanning for
thoughts.

 12.	Imagined that in my mind thoughts were coming down a conveyor belt; were boats on a
river; were train cars on a railroad track; were written on leaves flowing down a river; had
wings and could fly away; were clouds floating in the sky; or were going in and out of the
doors of my mind. (Underline the image you used.)

 13.	Other:
Describe thoughts you were mindful of during the week. State just each thought as it went through
your mind.
1. Thought:
Describe strategies you used or give numbers from above:
Circle how effective was this at helping you be more mindful and less reactive:
1
2
3
4
5
Not effective
Somewhat effective
Very effective
2. Thought:
Describe strategies you used or give numbers from above:
Circle how effective was this at helping you be more mindful and less reactive:
1
2
3
4
5
Not effective
Somewhat effective
Very effective
3. Thought:
Describe strategies you used or give numbers from above:
Circle how effective was this at helping you be more mindful and less reactive:
1
2
3
4
5
Not effective
Somewhat effective
Very effective

ourspace_kindness_pass.title: Mindfulness of Current Thoughts

main_doability_needs: bed-level or seated options by default
oxygen/breathing, pain, vertigo, fatigue, mobility, and recovery time respected
ADHD/autism supports: transitions, low-stimulation starts, tiny visible steps
partial completion and stopping before a crash count as success
no shame, no body blame, no pressure to push through pain or dysphoria
ADHD transition support before task demands
caregiver burnout recovery treated as care, not avoidance
protected rest, boundaries, decompression, sensory needs, and people-pleasing checks
no assumption that Jasper has unlimited caregiver capacity

household_rules: no hour block may hold more than two tasks/tools
skills may be used as one-breath or one-field versions
survival days load critical care first and skip optional worksheets
rest, boundaries, decompression, and asking for help count as care
skills are for support, not grades

universal_kindness_rules: This is a support tool, not a test.
One line, one checkbox, one breath, or one tiny step counts.
Skipping a skill because the body is unsafe, exhausted, dysregulated, or overloaded is allowed.
Do not stack this with more than one other task inside the same hour.
Survival-day version always wins over perfection.
william_accessibility_adaptations: Use bed-level, seated, voice-note, or caregiver-assisted versions when needed.
Avoid pain escalation, respiratory strain, unsafe transfers, and dysphoria-triggering language.
Break worksheets into one-field chunks; stopping before a crash is success.
jasper_accessibility_adaptations: Start with transition support before expecting focus.
Protect caregiver burnout recovery; rest and boundaries are valid completion.
Use body-doubling, timers, low-stimulation versions, and one-minute starts.
tiny_steps: Open the file without requiring completion.
Fill one field, circle one option, or read one prompt.
Stop, save, or print; partial completion counts.

routine_integration.start_the_day_use: eligible as a rotating daily skill, never more than one DBT/ADHD skill in an hour block
routine_integration.recommended_block: after body check or before decompression
